mnemonics.title: Catalan Number Formula
mnemonics.content: C(n) = (2n)! / (n! × (n+1)!) - Remember: '2n factorial over n factorial times n-plus-one factorial, divide by n-plus-one'.

mnemonics.title: Full Binary Tree Property
mnemonics.content: Leaves = Internal Nodes + 1 (L = I + 1). Memory: 'Full trees are balanced - leaves are just one more than internal nodes.'

mnemonics.title: Complete Binary Tree Height
mnemonics.content: Height = floor(log₂n). Memory: 'Complete trees keep it shallow, logarithmic is the path we crave.'

acronyms.usage: Used for efficient searching, insertion, deletion. Number of possible BST structures with n keys = Cₙ (Catalan number).
Traverses binary trees by exploring as deep as possible before backtracking. Used in pre-order, in-order, post-order traversals.
Stack-based traversal property used in recursive DFS and expression evaluation of binary trees.

visualTips.visualization: Draw the 5 trees for n=3: (1) root with left-left chain, (2) root with left-right chain, (3) root with left and right single children, (4) root with right-left chain, (5) root with right-right chain. Label each to remember the pattern.
Catalan numbers grow roughly as 4ⁿ/(n^(3/2)√π). Picture them as: C₀=1, C₁=1, C₂=2, C₃=5, C₄=14, C₅=42, C₆=132... They grow rapidly - the denominator's n^(3/2) slows growth compared to 4ⁿ.

Convert a Dyck path to binary tree: Each up-step adds a left child, each down-step returns to parent. Start at root, go up (left child), come down (back to parent), go up again (right child). The path never goes below baseline.

analogies.analogy: Think of building binary trees like constructing sentences. The root is the main subject, left subtree is one branch of description, right subtree is another. With n nodes, there are Cₙ ways to 'describe' your tree - like different sentence structures.

Catalan numbers are like the number of ways to fold a strip of paper, or the number of ways to triangulate a convex polygon - they appear everywhere in combinatorics but are specially connected to binary tree structures.

Complete is like a neatly filled classroom where you fill rows left-to-right. Full is like a family where every parent either has no children or exactly two - no single children allowed. Perfect trees satisfy both conditions.
